title: VPN Kill Switch Explained: How It Works and Which VPNs Have the Best One
description: A kill switch blocks all internet traffic if your VPN drops. Here's how different implementations work, which VPNs pass real tests, and which platforms have the best coverage.
content:
## What Is a Kill Switch and Why Does It Matter?

A VPN connection can drop. Servers go offline, your Wi-Fi hiccups, your laptop switches from mobile data to home broadband, or the VPN app crashes. When that happens, your operating system falls back to your regular internet connection, and your real IP address is suddenly exposed without any warning.

A kill switch prevents this. When the VPN connection drops, the kill switch immediately blocks all internet traffic until the VPN reconnects or until you manually disable it. The result: a brief connection interruption rather than a silent exposure.

This matters most for:
- Journalists and activists who rely on VPNs to conceal their location
- Torrent users who do not want their real IP appearing in peer swarms
- Anyone using public Wi-Fi on a connection that could drop unpredictably
- Users in countries with surveillance who need to ensure nothing bypasses the tunnel

## Two Types of Kill Switch

Most quality VPNs offer two variants:

**Standard kill switch:** Blocks traffic only when the VPN connection drops unexpectedly. If you manually disconnect the VPN, internet access resumes normally. This is the right mode for most users: you stay protected against accidental drops while keeping normal access when you intentionally turn the VPN off.

**Permanent (or advanced) kill switch:** Blocks all traffic unless the VPN is actively connected, including at system startup and when you manually disconnect. Even if you close the VPN app, internet access stays blocked until you reconnect. This mode is for users who need to ensure nothing ever bypasses the VPN under any circumstances.

## Kill Switch Implementations by Provider

### NordVPN

**Platforms:** Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS

NordVPN offers two levels on Windows and macOS:

- **Internet kill switch:** Blocks all traffic system-wide if the VPN drops. Network-level blocking means even system processes and background apps lose internet access.
- **App kill switch:** Lets you specify individual applications to block on disconnect. For example, block your torrent client but leave your browser running.

The app kill switch is particularly practical for users who want protection for specific use cases without cutting off everything. You configure a list of apps; those apps lose internet access if the VPN drops, everything else continues normally.

On **macOS**, NordVPN's kill switch activates on disconnect. Auto-reconnect is available on Windows, iOS, and Android but not on macOS, so a manual reconnect is required after a drop on that platform.

NordVPN passed independent kill switch testing in 2025–2026, with activation confirmed as near-instantaneous during simulated drops.

### ProtonVPN

**Platforms:** Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS

ProtonVPN's kill switch implementation is among the most thorough available:

**Standard kill switch:** Blocks traffic only on unexpected drops. Turning the VPN off manually restores normal access.

**Advanced (permanent) kill switch:** Blocks all internet traffic unless the VPN is actively connected, including during startup, shutdown, and manual disconnections. This is enforced at the OS firewall level on Windows and Linux, meaning it is not bypassable by disabling the VPN app.

On **Linux**, ProtonVPN's permanent kill switch is particularly well-implemented. It sets firewall rules that persist across reboots. Even if the VPN process is killed (not just disconnected), the firewall rules remain active. This is the closest implementation available to a hardware-level network lock.

On **Android**, ProtonVPN's kill switch behaves like the permanent mode by default, which is consistent with Android's OS-level VPN API behavior.

All ProtonVPN apps are open source, which means the kill switch implementation can be audited independently.

### Mullvad

**Platforms:** Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS

Mullvad's "block when disconnected" feature functions as a permanent kill switch and is one of the most hardened implementations available. The Mullvad app uses OS-level firewall rules rather than application-layer filtering, making it harder to bypass.

Mullvad also offers "LAN access" as a separate toggle, which lets you access local network devices (printers, NAS drives) while the kill switch is active without routing that traffic through the VPN. This is a practical detail that other providers often get wrong, either blocking LAN access entirely or leaving it fully open.

### ExpressVPN

**Platforms:** Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, routers

ExpressVPN calls their kill switch "Network Lock." It activates automatically when the VPN drops and blocks traffic until the connection is restored. In independent testing, Network Lock consistently activates within a fraction of a second of a disconnection.

ExpressVPN does not offer a permanent kill switch (one that also blocks when you manually disconnect). If you intentionally turn ExpressVPN off, internet access resumes normally. For most users this is fine. For high-security use cases, ProtonVPN or Mullvad's permanent modes are more appropriate.

### Surfshark

**Platforms:** Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS

Surfshark's kill switch passed independent testing in 2025. They offer a standard kill switch on all platforms. A permanent mode that blocks on manual disconnect is not available; this is the main gap compared to ProtonVPN and Mullvad.

## Kill Switch Testing: What Independent Tests Found

RTINGS.com and thebestvpn.com both conducted kill switch robustness testing across major VPN providers in 2025–2026. The methodology involved simulating VPN drops (process kill, network change, server timeout) and measuring whether traffic leaked in the gap between disconnection and kill switch activation.

Providers that passed with no detected leaks on all test scenarios: NordVPN, ProtonVPN, Mullvad, ExpressVPN, Surfshark.

Providers where leaks were detected in at least one scenario: several free VPNs and lower-tier paid services that advertise kill switches but implement them in software rather than at the OS firewall level. Software-level kill switches have a small activation delay; firewall-level implementations activate the moment the network state changes.

## Platform-Specific Considerations

### Windows

Windows is where kill switches have the most variation. OS-level implementations using Windows Filtering Platform (WFP) are the most reliable. NordVPN and ProtonVPN both use WFP on Windows. App-level kill switches (where the VPN app monitors the connection and calls firewall APIs reactively) have a measurable delay and can miss brief connection interruptions.

### macOS

macOS imposes stricter limits on third-party network extensions since macOS 11 (Big Sur). Most VPNs have adapted, but implementation quality varies. ProtonVPN and NordVPN both have solid macOS implementations. Auto-reconnect after a drop is less reliable on macOS than Windows across most providers.

### Linux

Linux users who care about kill switches should prefer ProtonVPN or Mullvad, both of which set iptables or nftables rules that persist at the system level. Many other providers offer a Linux CLI with limited kill switch support.

### Mobile (Android and iOS)

Both Android and iOS have OS-level "Always-on VPN" modes that function as permanent kill switches at the operating system level, regardless of which VPN app you use. On Android: Settings > Network > VPN > your VPN app > Always-on VPN and Block connections without VPN. On iOS: Settings > General > VPN & Device Management. Enabling this at the OS level is more reliable than relying on the VPN app's own kill switch on mobile.

## Recommendation

For most users: **NordVPN's** dual kill switch (system-level + app-level) covers the common scenarios well and the app kill switch is a genuinely useful feature. If you mainly want to protect a specific app (your torrent client, for example) rather than cut all traffic, the granularity is valuable.

For users with higher privacy requirements who need a permanent kill switch: **ProtonVPN's** permanent mode on Windows and Linux is the most thorough implementation from a widely-used commercial provider. The firewall-level enforcement and open-source code make it the most verifiable option.

For either provider, pair the kill switch with OS-level Always-on VPN on Android for mobile coverage.
